cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. So the question is asking why Doyle was surprised. The options are (A) to (D). Let me look at the passage again to make sure I understand. The story is about Arthur Conan Doyle, the writer of Sherlock Holmes, going on a trip in Europe. He gets into a taxi, and before he can say anything, the driver addresses him by name. Doyle is surprised and asks if the driver has seen him before, but the driver says no. Then the driver explains his reasoning based on clues like the newspaper, the taxi stop, Doyle's tan, ink spot on his fingers (indicating a writer), and his English clothes. Finally, the driver mentions that the name on the suitcase also gave it away. Now, the question is why was Doyle surprised. The key point is that the driver called him by his name before he had a chance to say anything. So initially, Doyle would think the driver recognized him, but the driver says he hadn't seen him before and deduced it through other clues. The surprise is that the driver knew his name. So looking at the options: A) the driver had seen him before. But the driver says he hadn't, so this is not correct. B) the driver could call him by his name. Yes, that's it. The driver addressed him by name without having met him before, so that's why Doyle was surprised. C) the driver was waiting for him. The driver wasn't necessarily waiting for him; he was at the taxi stop where people arriving from Marseille come. So the driver just assumed based on the clues, not that he was specifically waiting for Doyle. D) the driver answered no questions from him. No, the driver did answer questions when Doyle asked if he had seen him before. The driver did explain his reasoning, so this option is not right. So the answer should be B. The driver called him by his name without having met him before, which surprised Doyle. The driver's explanation follows, but the initial surprise is about being addressed by name.
